skills.title: Repeat a change with the dot command
skills.teach: The dot command (.) replays your last change — anything from entering Insert mode and typing, to a delete. The Dot Formula is the ideal: make the first change so it is repeatable, then it takes one keystroke to MOVE (j) and one to EXECUTE (.). Append with A so the cursor's column never matters.

challenges.why: I// is a repeatable change; one move + one execute comments each line.

challenges.why: Make the first change repeatable and the dot does the rest for free.

skills.title: Substitute only on matching lines (:g/re/s)

skills.title: Act inside each block (:g with a sub-range)
skills.teach: Ex commands under :g can take their OWN range, measured from the matching line. :g/{/ .+1,/}/-1 sort runs :sort on the lines BETWEEN each { and its } — alphabetising the body of every block in the file at once.

challenges.hint: for each {, sort the lines up to the matching }
challenges.why: A per-match sub-range lets :g reach inside every block.

challenges.hint: the same command sorts the body of EVERY block
challenges.why: One :g handles arbitrarily many blocks — it scales for free.

skills.title: Keyword autocompletion (<C-n>)
skills.teach: In Insert mode <C-n> completes the partial word before the cursor from words already in your buffers (<C-p> walks the list backward). Type enough to be unambiguous, press <C-n>, and Vim finishes a long identifier for you — no retyping, no typos.

challenges.hint: append to the partial word, then <C-n> to complete it

skills.title: Whole-line completion (<C-x><C-l>)
skills.teach: <C-x><C-l> completes an ENTIRE line that matches what you've typed so far, pulled from elsewhere in the buffer. Perfect for repeating a boilerplate line — a guard clause, an import — without copy-paste.

challenges.hint: start the line, then complete the whole thing
challenges.why: <C-x><C-l> echoes a matching line verbatim from the buffer.
